author: @JasonSheehan-fy3lb
text:
A shorter bit will give you less chatter in the cut.. also side milling is better than end milling as you have more cutting area so a deeper cut with a helix in with 50-70% tool diameter step over will give you a cleaner cut with less chatter .. I use the tape and ca glue trick to hold anything up to aluminum.. then I switch to toe clamps but make sure the clamp is slightly above level on the adjustment screw..
Correct Speeds and feeds , chip load and work holding are the key to success

author: @danblankenship5744
text:
Do you think the precision would be affected if the unit were operated upside down?
Dust and shavings wouldn't settle on the workpiece, so they wouldn't interfere with the cutting path.
You could add a skirt around the spindle to prevent it from getting coated.
Cleanup would also be easier since most of the debris would fall onto the floor—essentially, the inside of the old roof.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text: I hope some day to build a hybrid 3D metal printer+5-axis-mill.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
on the unit not having some stuff you just have gotten used to in the 3D printers I am guessing its a bit of unconscious eliteism for some companies of CNC equipment while others its just an over sight that is due to the industry being already thought through when it was not a thing, while 3D printing has been being done by new to industry thinkers who have also have this tech available to them right now and its affordable so it was fresh in their minds, so at some point I suspect unless 3D printing can replace the same level of manufacturing like my rust back into iron deposition for metal parts, then 3D printing companies will get into making CNC equipment and think of adding it, while CNC equipment manufacturers will get more into 3D printing and start thinking of adding the new niceties that the 3D printer industry has now taken for granted to their newer offerings eventually. 

Something I am waiting for standarization in 3D printers is closed loop operation where the back EMF from the motors will be collected and counted to let the machine know a step has been made fully vs it having no idea if it moved or someone is being a jerk and sitting there holding the head from moving while it is busy sending pulses to the weak little motors that 3D printers typically do not need more than (which are much faster response units too ,bigger means it takes more to make it respond as fast.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I suspect you are right mate

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
on going over this stuff for beginners and not being for experienced people? yeah I find I still learn stuff in CNC mill operation introduction videos especially these that I did not know and I have built my own and worked in machine shops running CNC equipment, so I would not discount the value of your work to even that pro, 

Its kinda the same thinking I find useful in repurposing known tech in manufacturing industry but in an unrelated sector to what I am doing. like high voltage charges, and lasers to eject the air particles from a given area temporarily for a deposition layer that requires a vacuum chamber normally (I like to think of it as a solid state vacuum chamber, vs the analog vacuum chambers we use to manufacture nearly all semiconductor tech tying us to the vacuum tube era still) 

Thank you seriously for putting these videos out on the carvera and because I have not stopped to remember it, all the other videos you have put out on even musings and the like, :)

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
did the laser probe come with it? because it sounds like you got a LOT more than even in 2017 prices I could have priced out for the same cost as what that unit sells for, I mean did the prices drop drastically or something? I paid I think nearly 1500 dollars for a unit with only 75mm height but 400 x 600mm capability, but it came with a really terrible driving system for the motors (cant really run it very fast if I were to want to use it for laser cutting or a 3D printer, plenty fast enough for a mill cutting through materials) and a bizarr USB to parallel port cable, 

things you got that I can see already, that did not come with mine? enclosure, vacuum cleaning system for cleaning up after itself, end stops (yeah zero end stops for mine LOL something if you want to run it from an arduino based 3D printer board you have to have or it will keep thinking it needs to home or something so forward movement moves it forward, backward movement....moves it forward yeah it wont do reverses with out that using a repurposed 3D printer board LOL 

that REALLY nice laser probe, the software already setup to use it and let you know all the things you need to do? yeah none of that came with mine either LOL

Like seriously looking into some similar stuff I can see would cost me more than another 1200 to 2000 dollars, and the software in the controller probably quite a bit more!!! 

TLDR, that unit comes packed with a TON of valuable stuff at that price point, really nice build from the company

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It did come with the laser probe and I agree it is a nice machine - i lack the ability to compare it against other machines as this is the only one I have ever used but it is a solid build, some  nice features and certainly interesting to use

author: @timmyssweet
text:
OGM, yeah, that turned me off CNC.  I just don't have a use for one.  Though, as a tool, they look brilliant.

In the future, I don't see metal doing anything high-temp filament can't.  I expect core filament, filament with long-strand CF core, to do A LOT.  

Of cause, if you need compression, for a sub for example, use a metal.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I was thinking about that - heat too - the very principle of FDM makes it unsuitable for building stuff for heat operations like engines for example - but I am with you - if you have no use for it don't buy it or it will just be a fine example of workshop art - having said that to my mind it opens possibilities too and extends you working capabilities

author: @davehorner420
text: I've always found the hardest part of desktop CNC is getting the feed rate and cutting speed correct for the material.  I believe you need to be careful when cutting aluminium; there are different grades and a soft aluminium can "smear" over the cutting tip, especially if the cutting speed is too high.  You might need to do a bit of research...?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I Agree it always depends on what you want to do and not all ali is the same - 2024 is good, 6061 one of the best all rounders for machinability thought 7070 and 7075 are said to be good

author: @CrudelyMade
text:
in makeraCAM when you choose the material you're working with aas well as the bit, and if it's a metal working bit, with metal settings, it should already work at more conservative cutting depths and speeds.  never hurts to check, but I'm 90% sure on this one. :-) 
I've seen recommendations in carvera CNC groups talking about going with a deeper depth of cut and a shallower step over.  but these commentors seem to have a better understanding of things like using multi flute bits and adjustments for better results.  why would that work that way? no idea.  take my word for it? please don't. :-D 
but.. if you happen to know a machinist or CNC operator in your area, it would be a fascinating discussion.  even more so if you share it. ;-) 
I bet visiting a local machinist might be good for the machinist as well, they get to talk about stuff that they hopefully like.. 
and..  I hear machinist and CNC operators might be gigs on the upswell in the next decade.

author: @RKH2OS
text: It is good practice to have the heel of the clamp slightly higher than the contact point of the part. Via heel block or screw through tapped hole at the end of the clamp.

replies.author: @mattw7949
replies.text: Solid guidance.  I'll add that your stud/bolt should be as close as possible to the work you are holding down.  Too close to the step block and you are holding that down more than your workpiece.
